title: He Kōrero Mō Te Awa: Voices of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream / Speaking for Our Stream: Sustainable Storytelling
subject: English
culturalContext: Students will explore the cultural and ecological significance of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream, a vital waterway flowing through our rohe. They will engage with local pūrākau (stories) of the stream, learn about its historical role in sustaining Ngāti Kahungunu communities, and examine current environmental challenges through both scientific data and mātauranga Māori perspectives.
learningObjectives: Analyze and compare contemporary environmental reports with traditional pūrākau to identify key themes about the health and significance of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream.
Compose a piece of creative writing (e.g., a poem, short narrative, or persuasive speech) that synthesizes research data and cultural narratives to advocate for the stream's protection.
Deliver an oral presentation, using effective verbal and non-verbal techniques, to share findings and proposed solutions with peers and invited community members.
Collaboratively create a digital 'Story Map' that layers written text, audio recordings, and images to tell the multifaceted story of the awa.

activities.title: Whakarongo: Listening to the Awa
activities.description: Students begin by visiting the Mangakōtukutuku Stream. They will silently observe and record sensory details (sights, sounds, smells). A local kaumātua will share the pūrākau of the stream, explaining its name and significance. Students will create a 'found poem' using words and phrases from the story and their observations.

activities.title: Titiro: Investigating the Current State
activities.description: In pairs, students will analyze the most recent Hawke's Bay Regional Council monitoring report for the Mangakōtukutuku Stream. They will use a graphic organizer to summarize key data points (e.g., water clarity, nitrate levels) and identify one primary environmental concern to focus on.

activities.title: Whakaaro: Weaving Knowledge Together
activities.description: Using a Venn diagram, students will compare the themes from the traditional pūrākau (e.g., life, sustenance, connection) with the themes emerging from the scientific data (e.g., pollution, fragility, change). In small groups, they will discuss the question: 'What is the core message both ways of knowing are telling us about our responsibility to the awa?'

activities.title: Kōrero: Giving Voice to the Awa
activities.description: Students will choose a creative form (poem, monologue, short story, or speech) to write from the perspective of the stream or a kaitiaki (guardian) of the stream. Their writing must integrate one specific element from the pūrākau and one specific piece of data from the environmental report to advocate for its protection.

activities.title: Tohatoha: Sharing Our Stories
activities.description: Students will contribute their writing to a collaborative digital Story Map (using a platform like ArcGIS StoryMaps or Google My Maps) that charts the journey of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream. They will present their final pieces to the class and invited whānau, explaining the choices they made in their writing.

resources: Kaumātua from local Ngāti Kahungunu marae to share pūrākau of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
Hawke's Bay Regional Council 'State of the Environment' monitoring reports for the Mangakōtukutuku catchment
Digital devices with internet access for creating ArcGIS or Google Story Maps
The book 'Ngā Pūrākau o Ngāti Kahungunu' by Dr. Jessica Hutchings and Dr. Jenny Lee-Morgan for additional cultural context

tasks: A written piece of creative writing that synthesizes cultural narrative and scientific data.
A digital contribution to the collaborative 'Story Map' of the awa.
A 2-3 minute oral presentation of their creative work to an audience.

nzcAlignment: English: Listening, Reading, and Viewing - Processes and strategies; Ideas; Language features
English: Speaking, Writing, and Presenting - Processes and strategies; Purposes and audiences; Ideas; Language features
Social Sciences: Understand how people participate individually and collectively in response to community challenges
Science: Living World - Ecology: Explain how the interaction between ecological factors and living things affects survival
